turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument now on number oh one four hundred, Ricky Bell versus Gary Bradford Cone. Mr. M- Mr. Moore.

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court The court of appeals was without authority to grant habeas relief under twenty-eight USC twenty-two fifty-four D one on respondent's ineffective assistance of counsel claim for two reasons: first, because the State court decision rejecting the claim correctly identified this Court's decision in Strickland versus Washington as the clearly established Federal law governing this case, not United States versus Cronic as respondent contends, and second, because the State court's application of Strickland to the facts of respondent's case was not objectively unreasonable. Turning to the first point, respondent's ineffective assistance claim from the outset of this case has asserted two specific errors that his attorney allegedly committed during the sentencing phase of his capital trial first, counsel's alleged failure to present available mitigating evidence and second, his counsel's decision to waive closing argument. This Court held in Strickland that such claims are properly analyzed under the two part actual deficient performance slash actual prejudice test announced in that case itself.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Mr. Moore, I think what happened in this sentencing proceeding, if I remember correctly, is that the attorney made some remarks at the beginning of the sentencing hearing?

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor. He delivered an opening statement, during which he specifically called the jury's attention, as he is permitted to do under Tennessee State law, to guilt phase evidence, mental health evidence, upon which he was relying in mitigation. He explained to the jury its mitigating significance by relating that evidence specifically to three statutory mitigating factors. In addition, during that opening statement, he emphasized his client's remorse for his role in the crimes. He emphasized his client's honorable service for his...

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: country. Did he...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: explain that he wouldn't be presenting any evidence or saying anything more?

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: He He did not indicate one way or the other in that statement,

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: but he specifically...

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Now, would you be here would you still be here if he had not said anything at the opening? Then what rule applies? Suppose the defense attorney just totally remained silent in the sentencing phase.

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: It is our...

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: It is our position that if the claim focuses on counsel's conduct during the trial and it is not alleged that any errors or omissions he made were the result of State interru- interference or so called surrounding circumstances, then such a claim is properly analyzed under Strickland's two part test.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: the err- the attorney here did successfully object to the presentation of some evidence during the sentencing phase,

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: didn't he?

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: indeed, Your Honor, he did.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: In addition,

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: he he objected to some hearsay evidence.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: J- just to go back to Justice O'Connor's question, suppose the attorney says nothing and la- later says, y- you know, I was just I don't know stressed out, traumatized.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: N- No, Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: When does Cronic apply?

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: Cronic in our view is properly read to apply only when surrounding circumstances or State interference renders it unlikely that any lawyer could have re- rendered effective assistance of counsel.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, in my hy- in my hypothetical, I said he he said I just blanked out for a minute.

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: But th- that circumstance is the the lawyer's own problem.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: ascertain whether that error preju-

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: given in this case that the attorney did provide that there was ineffective assistance at sentencing? Do we take that as a a gi-

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: No your honor No, Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: This is not the first time in one of these cases I've been surprised at how skimpy the presentation is at by the defense counsel on sentencing.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Well, Your Hon-

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: tempted to ask you if i- if this is usual, but th- th- that that's probably not a fair question is it there're so many differences in so many cases.

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: counsel when...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Moore, may I ask you? On that on that branch of it, it seems to me that there was, the prosecutor's presentation to the jury was about a match for the defense attorney's.

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: Y- Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: But I had another question that I wanted to ask you, and that was you presented two questions.

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: Yes, Your...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: As a matter and and the Sixth Circuit proceeded just the other way.

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: Well, it's our it's our position, Your Honor, that it is not the function of this Court under twenty-two fifty-four D to reach to actually address the merits a- as if it were deciding this claim de novo.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Don't you think it'd be a little coy for us to decide, well, it wasn't an unreasonable application of of Federal law when we, in fact, know or believe that it was a correct application of Federal law? Do you insist that we simply say and go no further than to say, oh, it was it was not an unreasonable er application?

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: I I certainly would not b- begrudge the Court's agreeing that the State court had indeed correctly applied Strickland.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: ascertaining...

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: whether it is reasonable.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Indeed, if we could go no further than than the coy statement that it was not an unreasonable application, I suppose you shouldn't've had two questions.

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: argument...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: le- let me interrupt you.

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: And so, i- i- in- indeed, counsel could've reasonably believed that all of the points he had made during his opening statement, his plea for mercy, his emphasis on his client's remorse, and the mitigating significance of the guilt phase evidence, were fresh in the jury's mind when the jury retired to deliberate because...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: that...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: what did he say about the Bronze Star?

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: During his opening statement, he did not specifically mention the Bronze Star because that evidence was not elicited until the his cross examination of one of the witnesses during the sentencing phase.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: So, your answer is he didn't mention it in his argument.

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: He did not mention the Bronze Star.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: But am I right that such evidence was introduced in the Federal court?

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: none of that evidence...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: that such evidence was available made in the Federal proceeding?

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: The allegation that it was available was made in the Federal proceeding, but there was no...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: And was that...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: allegation denied?

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Yeah.

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: Well, I don't know that it was denied.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: on...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: but if we're not deciding the case on the basis of what that evidence would prove or disprove, but rather on whether counsel was deficient in failing to introduce it, should we not assume the evidence exists?

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: We should not?

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: If If...

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: b- b- because the burden rests with the petitioner, the habeas petitioner, to demonstrate its existence.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: What what was the bas- what was the case you just cited to us for that proposition?

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: Burger versus Kemp.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Mr. Moore, er are you done with that point? I...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Yes, sir.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I- It isn't stated in your brief, but I assume that it's it's it's the Tennessee rule that if if the defense doesn't make a closing a closing statement, the prosecution doesn't either.

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: That's right, and that was the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: s- s-

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: the State court found, based on the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: evidence...

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: presented to it during the post conviction hearing that counsel made a tactical decision to waive in in order to prevent the senior prosecutor from delivering what he...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: The fearsome Mr. Strother.

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: I am not I am not acquainted with General Strother, so I'm not...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: sure, Your Honor.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: gather that this isn't the only occasion on which defense counsel have esch- eschewed the making of closing argument for fear that Mr. Strother would be enabled to unleash his his weaponry.

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Can we go back to the Bronze Star? It certainly did come out in the sentencing phase.

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: Do we have any evidence to indicate whether Mr. Dice would have put on evidence showing the Bronze Star if he had not been able to bring it out in cross examination?

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: The record simply doesn't reflect whether or not that would've been the case.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Do you think he was entitled to be pleased with himself for the way he got that in the record?

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: Your Honor, I th-

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: That is that the way you would have done it if you'd been the lawyer?

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: I'm not certain that I that th- hearsay evidence in a prison record is the best evidence of that fact.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: that's all the jury found out about it, too.

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: th- And we know nothing more about it now? Nothing came out in the Tennessee proceedings...

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: No, Your Honor,

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: and r- and and r-

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: respondent introduced no evidence before the State courts concerning why th- the the Bronze Star was awarded, anything about the circumstances of its award.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Was it were there any problems in his service record? He served in Germany and Vietnam.

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: Not so insofar as this record reflects, Your Honor, no.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: And did the lawyer put in any evidence about his what kind of a person he was before he went to Vietnam?

turns.name: MICHAEL E. MOORE
turns.text: During sentencing? No.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Moore.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Blatt, we'll hear from you.

turns.name: LISA S. BLATT
turns.text: [NS] Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court The United States has addressed the second question presented, which is whether a defendant must show prejudice to establish a claim of ineffective assistance.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: LISA S. BLATT
turns.text: to assume then that the performance was deficient and then address the prejudice prong? No.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: don't get to question two at all, then.

turns.name: LISA S. BLATT
turns.text: You get to you can ge- you get to question two if, in determining that Strickland and not Cronic is the clearly established law, this Court holds that Cronic does not apply when the claim is an actual ineffective assistance claim.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: What what if the defense counsel presented nothing at all at the sentencing phase, do you think that there is potential for application of Cronic in those circumstances?

turns.name: LISA S. BLATT
turns.text: Yes, Justice O'Connor.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: in that re-

turns.name: LISA S. BLATT
turns.text: We do but our difference is very narrow.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Why divide trial into, you know, the one phase and then the the mitigation phase? It's all part of the same trial.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: and say...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: counsel utterly failed to to litigate this particular piece.

turns.name: LISA S. BLATT
turns.text: Okay, and We couldn't agree with you more.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Well, what justification do you have for for separating out the mitigation phase from the other, especially when some of the evidence that went to mitigation was presented during during the direct phase?

turns.name: LISA S. BLATT
turns.text: Right.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: you're giving up the principle.

turns.name: LISA S. BLATT
turns.text: We don't think...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: so, what...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: it just be th- th- here's a counsel who litigated the case properly but he made he made a mistake in in in his litigation? He didn't put on any evidence in the in the mitigation phase.

turns.name: LISA S. BLATT
turns.text: We don't think so.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: be the result of any...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: when you say the entire trial, are you talking about the penalty phase or the whole ca- the whole trial?

turns.name: LISA S. BLATT
turns.text: We would be talking about a penalty phase although I agree with Justice Scalia that in the unique situation of this case, where there's an express procedure that allows the jury to consider the mitigation evidence, it's critical to look at counsel's performance during the g- the guilt phase of the trial.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Counsel...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: me.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: the...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: penalty phase?

turns.name: LISA S. BLATT
turns.text: No, I don't think that is unique and I I'm sorry I...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I misspoke. I think it's...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Which means the two phases are linked.

turns.name: LISA S. BLATT
turns.text: Right, and if you d- if we're just talking if you take it out of the capital proceeding so you don't have the split trial, all we're saying is if there's an entire failure, we would think that it would be appropriate to presume prejudice.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Why isn't the line is it know, that you can draw a line one place doesn't mean it's sensible to draw it every place.

turns.name: LISA S. BLATT
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: So,

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: w- one can say yes I see these are two parts it doesn't follow from that that I have to then separate every examination and every cross examination It's a question of where you draw the line.

turns.name: LISA S. BLATT
turns.text: Well, I think that's correct, and however you draw the line, this case falls on the Strickland side of the line because this is not a case where counsel didn't do anything.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: May I ask? Supposing you had a case and I know this is not quite it in which there is strong evidence that counsel was mentally disabled and that that made him less effective throughout the entire sentencing hearing.

turns.name: LISA S. BLATT
turns.text: It would be under Strickland.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Even if...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: there was severe mental illness on the part of counsel?

turns.name: LISA S. BLATT
turns.text: Is counsel's j- things that would go to impair counsel judgments or generally irrelevant unless they manifest themself manifest themselves in objectively unreasonable conduct.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: because...

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: treat the failure to make a closing statement or the failure to put in any evidence whatsoever e- e- exactly as if he were a fully qualified lawyer in such a case.

turns.name: LISA S. BLATT
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: presumption of prejudice.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: Burger versus Kemp if the district court in this case had wanted to inquire about the availability of other evidence, it was precluded from doing so because it hadn't been introduced in the State collateral proceedings?

turns.name: LISA S. BLATT
turns.text: ye- The district court ruled that that that those claims were procedurally barred under an adequate and independent State procedure.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: proceedings...

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: procedural bar rule if the evidence is not adduced at the State collateral proceeding, as opposed to Federal deference?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: It was a procedural...

turns.name: LISA S. BLATT
turns.text: In this context, the claims were not made until subsequent post conviction State court proceedings.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: barred.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: was just the failure to adduce the to to make the claim rather than to elicit the evidence?

turns.name: LISA S. BLATT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Ms. Blatt.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court The main problem in this case is not specific attorney errors.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well, he did say something initially, did he not, at the sentencing phase?

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Justice O'Connor, he did but I would like to clear up one thing that Mr. Moore stated.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: One...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: Well I just I hope you will address that because if he actually did something but it was somehow inadequate assistance, then perhaps Strickland is the test.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: But if...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: he did absolutely nothing, then we have to wrestle with whether you divide it from, you know, sentencing phase from guilt innocence phase, and so forth.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Justice O'Connor, Mr. Dice did make an opening statement, but in that opening statement, he also told the jury that he had a right to put on evidence at the penalty phase and had a right to make a closing argument in the penalty phase.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: And it's really bad performance, you can argue, having especially having made that statement, not to do it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: But But...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I don't think that it proves that he wasn't there.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Justice Scalia, the problem in this case and the reason there's a total abdication of advocacy is because after the ca- State made a case for death, after the State put on proof of aggravating circumstances and then argued to the jury that the law required the jury to put Mr. Cone to death, there was silence.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: Well, he had he had asked questions on cross examination, and it may be that he was satisfied that the State hadn't shown much.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: He wasn't...

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: going to give them an opportunity to have some stem winder and and and this is not standard but it is is it is not an unknown strategy.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: It was a...

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: very effective strategy for that particular advocate.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Justice Kennedy, the only role of an advocate in the penalty phase of a capital trial is to make a case for life.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: Or it implies to the jury that the State has shown nothing.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That's...

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: a completely permissible inference, and counsel does that has been known to do that.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: W- We have counsel up here sometimes who say I I waive rebuttal.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Justice Scalia, if a member of the Court asked a question of counsel and counsel stood silent, the necessary implication of that is I have no good reply for that question.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Mr.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Hutton, this was a very short proceeding.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Yes,

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: It shows that Mr. Dice did did something.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, I would submit we know that Mr. Dice was suffering from mental illness at the time he testified.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: I'm asking you about those words.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Y- Your Honor, I think e- I would submit that that's more of a statement of grandiosity, but even if it was a plea for mercy, our position is that after the opening statements, nothing happened to make a case for life.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: W- Why W- Th- I'd like you to go into that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: exactly the...

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: same reaction as Justice Ginsburg.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Justice Breyer, there there're two points in response.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: In other words,

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: you're saying on that part...

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: when Paul Freund sometimes has said, a lawyer in this Court who sits down saying nothing makes not just a good argument, but a perfect argument.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: My reading this transcript led me to think maybe it wasn't the perfect response, but it was a good one, because in the introductory statement I'll repeat myself he made all his arguments.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Number one, Your Honor, it's not just the failure to make a statement.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: conviction...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: me.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: penalty phase.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: allude to that evidence?

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: mitigating ev-

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: but surely the jury would assume that they could consider it if he referred to it.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Your Honor, for a jury who's just rejected an insanity defense and this will Justice Scalia, this plays into the second point, too.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: pro-

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: He says the defense has put on proof of those mitigating circumstances during its case.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Justice Breyer, ther- the problem with that evidence was that the post conviction court made a finding there was prosecutorial misconduct where the prosecution improperly argued that the jury should not believe the evidence with respect to drug usage.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: phase.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: you go to I don't want you to lose two, though.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: Because I want to elicit from you what your response is.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: Well I, you know, I have trouble with that because I don't think the State put on very much, and if I'm sitting waiting for this closing argument, I know what I'm going to hear.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Hm.

turns.name: ANTHONY M. KENNEDY
turns.text: But then the prosecutor says, this man with a Bronze Star killed a helpless, innocent couple.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: got me...

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: But Justice Kennedy, at least then the jury has something to weigh.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: All right.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: No.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Justice Breyer, put another way, the question the Court is asking is whether or not a lawyer can strategically decide to abandon advocacy.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: ah- All he has...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: abandoned...

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: is his closing statement.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: No, Justice Breyer.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: No, but Mr. Hutton, the problem that I think we're all having with your argument i- is is illustrated by the the colloquy that keeps going on.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Justice Souter, it's our position that, in essence, the lawyer's judgments are irrelevant to a Cronic analysis, that Cronic looks at the...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: structure of...

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: the whole problem, because if we were analyzing it under Strickland, we would have a different sort of inquiry, and maybe it fits better here.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: No, Justice...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: O'Connor.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: would happen then? And how should we address it with these two questions? Do we deal with twenty-two fifty-four first as a threshold question?

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Justice O'Connor, if I could take both of your questions in the order you presented them.

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: You know I I...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: I-

turns.name: SANDRA DAY O'CONNOR
turns.text: It's h- It puts you in a bad position for me to ask you this, but just assume, if you would for a minute, that we think Strickland applies.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: your client?

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Justice O'Connor, the if Strickland did apply, the twenty-two fifty-four D question could not be resolved until first the procedural default issues and the failure to afford an evidentiary hearing questions are resolved by the Sixth Circuit.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: we not decide the toi- the preju- the Strickland question here?

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: What what were those...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Were these questions dealing with the guilt phase or the penalty phase?

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Your Honor, these are all questions that apply to application of Sixth Amendment, the failure to develop...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: evidence...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: I I asked you a specific question.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: With respect to the penalty phase specifically, Chief Justice Rehnquist, with respect to developing proof as to deficient performance, why the findings of fact should not be trusted because ment-

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: me-  mis-

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: didn't the Sixth Circuit reject your claim about the guilt phase?

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: e- No Your what they rejected, there was a- an issue of waiver of certain claims, not the Sixth Amendment claims, but other claims that was denied by the district court, and the Sixth Circuit found that those issues were waived.

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: Can you can you point to me where in the Sixth Circuit opinion I thought in their opinion they rejected your claim insofar as the guilt phase.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: No, Justice Ginsburg.

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Excuse m- I I I don't I don't really understand.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Because...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: I don't understand why we can't just just look at the evidence that was available and and decide the the Strickland question then.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Justice Scalia, that fits right into this court's decision with Michael Williams versus Taylor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Justice...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: any indiction here as to whose fault it was?

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: We Your Honor, there's several different issues, Justice Souter.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: under Michael Williams versus...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: facts is it what facts is it are are they that that you sought to develop?

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Ch- Chief...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: bearing on the ineffective assistance claim?

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Chief Justice, there's several facts, starting with the deficient performance aspect.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: law f-

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: W- W- Why doe- how does that bear on whether or not the State court's finding of against you on the Strickland claim was unreasonable?

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Because, Chief Justice, many of the findings by the State court relied on the testimony of John Dice, and just like under the old Townsend versus Sain...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: and...

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Townsend against Sain is pretty well gone.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: No, Your Honor, but it also comes into Michael Williams versus Taylor, this Court's two thousand term two th- year two thousand opinion, where if it's not if we didn't fail to develop facts in in State court that are relevant, we can develop them in Federal court.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: What is what is the particular thing, though, because I mean, you've mentioned three times now that he has some mental problem that led him to commit suicide.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: gather that must have been at least four or five years after these events.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: It was a-

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: testified at the hearing in nineteen eighty-six.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: Now, I've seen many bad cases of bad representation in death cases that I think is terrible.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: e- Justice Breyer, with respect to your first question, we're raising the fact that he committed suicide after the post conviction testimony so that that imp- that qu-

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: raises...

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: does not suggest that four or five years earlier it might suggest a cause of bad representation, but it doesn't suggest there was the bad representation.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Justice Breyer, he failed to make a case for life.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: you with this? And I because I think we're all trying to get at the same thing.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: With resp- Justice Souter, there're m- many cases for life that could have been made.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Number tw--

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: was there nothing in the military record that the lawyer might have been fearful about if he pursued that beyond where he did?

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: No, Justice Ginsburg.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: So...

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: just m- I've looked back at the opinion, and twice the Sixth Circuit says that they deny the they affirm the dismissal.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: No, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: We di-

turns.name: RUTH BADER GINSBURG
turns.text: you t- you told me when I asked you, no, the Sixth Circuit didn't affirm with respect to the sentence of conviction.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: phase.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: I'm sorry, Justice Ginsburg.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: petition.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: UNKNOWN JUSTICE
turns.text: phase...

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: of the capital...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Mr.

turns.name: JOHN PAUL STEVENS
turns.text: Could I ask you one question following up on Justice Souter? He listed three things.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: N- no, Your Your Honor, there were some references by Mr. Dice's testimony that he thought that the mother, Valeree Cone, did not make a good witness and that generally the family members he thought did not make a good witness.

turns.name: STEPHEN G. BREYER
turns.text: that come in during the dur- had that come in during the guilt stage?

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Your Honor, vera- th- th- he at- when he tried to introduce evidence in the guilt phase, there were objections as to relevancy which were sustained by the court, because the court found that all that was relevant in the guilt phase was the issue of mental insanity.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: particular...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: mother testify in the...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: to send th- th- th- this conviction and sentence occurred in nineteen eighty-two.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Your Honor, first of a- this case was...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: filed...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: How old is is is Mr. Cone?

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Mr. Cone was thirty-three in nineteen-eighty-two, so that would make him fifty...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: Yeah, well, fifty-thr- he may he may get a...

turns.name: ANTONIN SCALIA
turns.text: life sentence by default.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Justice Scalia, though the the the fact of the matter th- is, though, that Mr. Cone has been trying to develop these...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: claims.

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: but when you go back to that, what is it that you that you say should have gone in as evidence at the sentencing phase that didn't? The Bronze Star.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Your Honor, that should've gone in.

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: Mr. Dice's...

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: What what about the other two that I mentioned, the the the death of the sister and the murder of of the the the the girlfriend? Should those things have gone in?

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, because they would...

turns.name: UNKNOWN SPEAKER
turns.text: portray par-

turns.name: DAVID H. SOUTER
turns.text: I'm pushing you because your time is running out.

turns.name: ROBERT L. HUTTON
turns.text: Your Honor, those should've gone in, but more importantly, those should've been woven into an argument as to why the reasoned moral judgment a reasoned moral judgment called for this man not to be put to death.

turns.name: WILLIAM H. REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you.
